key_insight: Random solid-solution and local chemical order in WTaCrV change how radiation damage and deformation evolve, with both configurations suppressing mechanical degradation despite forming more primary defects than the average-atom model.
engineering_relevance: For reactor-facing refractory alloys, nanoscale chemical disorder and ordering can improve irradiation tolerance and help retain strength under extreme nuclear environments.
method: Atomistic simulations with newly developed interatomic potentials
Compare Average-Atom (AA), random solid-solution (RSS), and local chemical order (LCO) configurations
Run collision-cascade and deformation analyses to track Frenkel pairs, twins, and flow stress
evidence_signal: At the same radiation dose, Frenkel-pair production follows NRSS > NLCO > NAA, while irradiation strongly degrades the AA model but has limited impact on strength and flow stress in RSS and LCO.
watch_item: The conclusions are based on atomistic modeling, so transferability to real alloys depends on how well the potentials and idealized chemical-order states represent experimental microstructures.
paper_title: Primary damage and mechanical degradation of WTaCrV refractory high-entropy alloy: effects of solid-solution and chemical ordering
